title: Study on the validity of IPT+parquet method as an impurity solver in DMFT focusing on orbital fluctuations
authors: Aira Yamada, Ryota Mizuno, Masayuki Ochi, Kazuhiko Kuroki, Takuma Ohashi
abstract: A breakdown of calculations with exact impurity solvers in the dynamical mean field theory in multiband systems easily occurs due to the expensive numerical cost. To overcome this practical difficulty, three of the present authors developed an inexpensive and reliable impurity solver by combining the iterative perturbation theory (IPT) and parquet equation, and named it IPT+parquet [R. Mizuno, et al., Phys. Rev. B 104, 035160 (2021).]. In this study, we validate IPT+parquet focusing on the orbital fluctuation by comparing the numerically exact impurity solvers. We confirm that IPT+parquet can capture competition between orbital fluctuation channels, which the conventional IPT cannot capture.
summary: This paper rigorously validates the IPT+parquet method as an advanced impurity solver for DMFT in multi-orbital systems. It demonstrates that this combined technique accurately captures subtle physics, particularly orbital fluctuations, which simpler methods fail to resolve. The work provides a reliable computational tool for studying strongly correlated materials beyond simple Hubbard models.
main_problem: The study aims to validate the IPT+parquet method as a computationally inexpensive yet accurate impurity solver for Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT), specifically focusing on its ability to capture orbital fluctuations in multiband systems.
main_result: IPT+parquet successfully captures competition between orbital fluctuation channels, which conventional IPT fails to describe. The inclusion of the parquet contribution is crucial for accurately modeling these multi-orbital Mott physics effects.
method: The authors compare results from IPT+parquet against numerically exact solvers (like CT-QMC) and simpler approximations by analyzing the self-energy calculation within DMFT.
model_or_system: The research uses a multiband Hubbard model, applied to two-orbital systems on both square lattices and Bethe lattices. The Hamiltonian includes hopping integrals and various Coulomb repulsion terms ($U, U', J, J'$).
key_observables: Quasiparticle weight ($Z_\alpha$), which probes correlation effects; band-filling ($n_\alpha$).
important_parameters: Interaction strengths ($U, U'$) and the ratio between them relative to Hund coupling ($J$); the system is studied near half-filling.
main_assumptions: The method relies on combining Iterative Perturbation Theory (IPT) with the parquet equation. Modifications were necessary for stability when interaction parameters deviate from specific relationships (e.g., $U' = U -2J$).
figures_summary: Figures compare the quasiparticle weight ($Z$) as a function of filling or interaction strength across multiple methods (Exact, IPT+parquet, conventional IPT), demonstrating where agreement is achieved and where failures occur.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the theoretical framework (DMFT, Parquet, IPT) and then validates IPT+parquet by applying it to two-orbital models on both square and Bethe lattices. Subsequent sections detail necessary modifications to the parquet calculation for improved stability.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter strongly correlated physics, the development of robust, scalable numerical solvers like IPT+parquet is a general computational tool relevant to any field requiring complex many-body simulations (e.g., quantum chemistry or AMO systems with strong interactions).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The decomposition of the full vertex. The full vertex can be divided into four parts: the fully irreducible part (Λ) and the reducible parts (Φl, l = ph, ph, pp). The reducible vertices Φl can be divided into two parts by cutting a pair of Green’s functions as indicated by the red dashed lines.
FIG. 2. The noninteracting density of states of the models. Left and right panels show the density of states of two-orbital square lattice and two-orbital Bethe lattice, respectively.

FIG. 3. The band filling n dependence of the quasiparticle weight Z of the two-orbital square lattice model. The temperature is T/t = 0.2 and the on-site energy difference is δ/t = 1.6. Triangle, circle, as- terisk, and square symbols (purple, blue, khaki, and green lines) rep- resent IPTn=n0, IPTnα=n0α, IPT+parquet, and CT-QMC, respectively. Top, middle, and bottom panels show the quasiparticle weight Z at U/t = 4, 6, 10, respectively. The left and right panels indicate orbital 1 and orbital 2, respectively.

FIG. 4. The interaction strength (U, U′) dependence of the quasiparticle weight Z of the two-orbital Bethe lattice model obtained by (a) Exact diagonalization, (b) IPT+parquet, and (c) IPT, respectively. The Hund coupling and pair hopping are fixed as J = J′ = 0. The temperatures are (a) T/W = 0 and (b), (c) T/W = 0.005, respectively. Left figure is taken from Ref. [12].

FIG. 5. The interaction strength (U, U′) dependence of the quasi- particle weight Z of the two-orbital Bethe lattice model obtained by IPT+parquet. The Hund coupling is (a) J/W = 0.7 and (b) J/W = −0.7, respectively. The pair hopping is J′ = 0. The tem- perature is T/t = 0.005.
